title: EXPOSÉ XVII LEFSCHETZ PENCILS: EXISTENCE THEOREM

pages.title: EXPOSÉ XVII LEFSCHETZ PENCILS: EXISTENCE THEOREM

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ XVII
LEFSCHETZ PENCILS: EXISTENCE THEOREMby N. M. KATZ
0. Introduction

In the present exposé (which, in the oral seminar, was placed before the exposés XIV, XV devoted to the Picard-Lefschetz formula), we introduce the "Lefschetz pencils" of hyperplane sections of a smooth projective scheme, and we give conditions for their existence; in the following exposé, we make an elementary cohomological study of them, based on the known structure of the cohomology of blown-up varieties. The fact that certain hypotheses on Lefschetz pencils which we are led to introduce are often satisfied will follow from the Picard-Lefschetz formula of Exp. XV, of a less elementary nature than the results of the present exposé and the following one.

Certain developments of the present exposé and the following one will be found expounded elsewhere in a more general framework (general base schemes, quasi-projectivity hypotheses instead of projectivity, etc.), notably the pre-cohomological study of pencils of hyperplane sections (and of Lefschetz pencils more particularly) in EGA V, and the cohomological study of blown-up schemes in the exposé of JOUANOLOU SGA 5 VII; the reader will also find in EGA V proofs in which intrinsic diagram chasing replaces the use of coordinates, of which the writer of the present exposé is fond.

I wish to thank P. DELIGNE and P. GRIFFITHS for the help they gave me in the conception of this exposé. Throughout this exposé, \(k\) denotes an algebraically closed field, unless expressly mentioned otherwise.

pages.title: 1. A reminder on quadratic singularities (cf VI 6).

blocks.html: 1. A reminder on quadratic singularities (cf VI 6).
1.1. Let \(Y\) be a \(k\)-scheme purely of dimension \(n\). A closed point \(y\in Y\) is called an ordinary quadratic singularity of \(Y\) if:

a) the completion \(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{Y,y}\) of the local ring of \(y\) in \(Y\) is isomorphic to the quotient \(k[[T_1,\ldots,T_n]]/(Q(T))\) of the formal power series in \(n=\dim_k Y\) variables by the ideal generated by an element \(Q(T)\);

b) \(Q\) begins only in degree two, and the subvariety of \(\mathbb P_k^{n-1}\) defined by the vanishing of the homogeneous part of degree two of \(Q(T)\) is smooth over \(k\) if \(n\geq2\), and the homogeneous part of \(Q(T)\) of degree two is non-zero if \(n=1\).
If the characteristic of \(k\) is different from two, or if \(n\) is even, condition b) is equivalent to the condition b’) \(Q\) begins only in degree two, and the Hessian matrix of \(Q\)
\[ \left(\frac{\partial^2Q}{\partial T_i\partial T_j}(0)\right)_{1\leq i,j\leq n} \]
is invertible.
As one sees by relying on the Jacobian criterion of smoothness.
The "standard form" for the homogeneous part of degree two \(Q_2\) of a series \(Q\) satisfying b), a form which can be obtained after a linear change of variable, is

\[ \tag{1.1.1} \begin{cases} Q_2(T)=\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{[n/2]}T_{2i-1}T_{2i},&\text{if }n\text{ is even},\\[6pt] Q_2(T)=T_n^2+\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{[(n-1)/2]}T_{2i-1}T_{2i},&\text{if }n\text{ is odd}, \end{cases} \]
as one sees by the theory of quadratic forms (Bourbaki, Alg. X).

1.2. A closed point \(y\in Y\) which satisfies a), b) is called a non-degenerate quadratic singularity. As the determinant of the Hessian matrix is identically zero in characteristic two if \(n\) is odd, non-degenerate quadratic singularities in dimension \(n\) exist only if either \(n\) is even, or \(k\) is of characteristic different from two, cases in which they are exactly the ordinary quadratic singularities.

pages.title: 2. Lefschetz pencils: statement of the results

blocks.html: 2. Lefschetz pencils: statement of the results

2.1. Let us denote by \(\mathbb P^r\) the projective space of \(r\) dimensions over \(k\), by \(\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}\) the dual projective space of hyperplanes in \(\mathbb P^r\), and by \(\operatorname{Gr}(1,\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r})\) the variety of lines in \(\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}\). A point \(D\in\operatorname{Gr}(1,\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r})\) is called a pencil of hyperplanes in \(\mathbb P^r\). Considering \(D\) as a line in \(\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}\), we write, by abuse of notation, \(D=\{H_t\}_{t\in D}\), the \(H_t\) being the hyperplanes of \(\mathbb P^r\) which are the points of the line \(D\). We call the axis of \(D\) the linear subvariety of \(\mathbb P^r\) of codimension two which is the intersection \(H_o\cdot H_\infty\) of any two distinct elements of the pencil. Considering the axis of \(D\) as a point of \(\operatorname{Gr}(r-2,\mathbb P^r)\), the arrow \(D\longmapsto\) the axis of \(D\) is none other than the well-known orthogonality isomorphism
\[ \operatorname{Gr}(1,\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r})\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Gr}(r-2,\mathbb P^r). \]

2.2. Let \(X\) be a \(k\)-scheme, proper, smooth and irreducible, of dimension \(n\geq1\), equipped with an embedding \(X\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow\mathbb P^r\). A pencil \(D=\{H_t\}_{t\in\mathbb P^1}\) of hyperplanes in \(\mathbb P^r\) is called a Lefschetz pencil (with respect to the embedding \(X\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow\mathbb P^r\)) if
a) the axis of \(D\) cuts \(X\) transversally (EGA IV 17.13.7);
b) there exists a dense open set \(U\) of \(D\) such that for every \(t\in U\), the hyperplane \(H_t\) cuts \(X\) transversally;
c) for \(t_o\in D-U\), \(H_{t_o}\) cuts \(X\) transversally except at one point, which is an ordinary quadratic singular point of \(X\cdot H_{t_o}\).
We shall see below (3.2.1) that the Lefschetz pencils form an open set of \(\operatorname{Gr}(1,\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r})\).
2.3. The embedding \(X\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow\mathbb P^r\) is called a Lefschetz embedding if the Lefschetz pencils form a dense open set in \(\operatorname{Gr}(1,\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r})\)*).

2.4. Let us now recall the notion of a multiple of a given embedding \(X\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow\mathbb P^r\). It is the composite of the given embedding \(X\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow\mathbb P^r\) and of the \(d\)-th Segre embedding,
\[ S_d:\mathbb P^r\longrightarrow\mathbb P^{\binom{r+d}{d}-1} \]
which is written in terms of the homogeneous coordinates \(X_o,\ldots,X_r\) in \(\mathbb P^r\)
\[ (X_o,\ldots,X_r)\xrightarrow{S_d}(\ldots,X^w,\ldots) \]

where \(X^w=X_o^{w_o}\cdots X_r^{w_r}\) runs through the monomials of degree \(d\) in \(X_o,\ldots,X_r\). Let us remark that a "section of \(X\) by a hypersurface of degree \(d\)"**) for a given embedding is none other than a
"hyperplane section" for the \(d\)-th multiple of this embedding.
Theorem 2.5. Let \(X\) be irreducible, proper and smooth over \(k\) of dimension \(n\geq1\), equipped with an embedding \(i:X\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow\mathbb P^r\). Then
2.5.1. Every \(d\)-th multiple of \(i\), with \(d\geq2\), is a Lefschetz embedding.
2.5.2. If \(k\) is of characteristic zero, the given embedding \(i\) is a Lefschetz embedding.
The proof breaks up into several pieces, which follow.

footnotes.html: Or more correctly, in the set of closed points of \(\operatorname{Gr}(1,\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r})\). We shall allow ourselves such abuses of language in what follows.
In English, one says more conveniently: hypersurface section of degree \(d\).

blocks.html: 3. The dual variety

3.1. Let \(I\) be the Ideal in \(\mathbb P^r\) which defines \(X\). We denote by \(N\) the conormal sheaf \(I/I^2\), a locally free sheaf of rank \(r-n\) on \(X\), and by \(\mathbb P(N)\) the projective bundle over \(X\) formed by the lines in \(\check N\). (For \(X=\mathbb P^r\), \(\mathbb P(N)\) is empty.)

The bundle \(\mathbb P(N)\) is identified with the subvariety of \(X\times\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}\) formed by the pairs \((x,H)\) such that \(H\) is tangent to \(X\) at \(x\), via the arrow \(\mathbb P(N)\xrightarrow{\nu}X\times\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}\)
\[ \tag{3.1.1} \mathbb P_x(I/I^2)\ni F\xmapsto{\nu}\left(x,\text{ the hyperplane with equation }\sum_i\frac{\partial F}{\partial X_i}(x)T_i\right), \]
\(F\) denoting a local section of \(I/I^2\). We define
\[ \tag{3.1.2} \varphi:\mathbb P(N)\longrightarrow\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r} \]
as being the composite \(\mathbb P(N)\xrightarrow{\nu}X\times\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}\xrightarrow{\operatorname{pr}_2}\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}\).

3.1.3. We denote by \(\check X\) the image of \(\varphi\); it is the set of hyperplanes which do not cut \(X\) transversally, and is called the dual variety of \(X\) (embedded in \(\mathbb P^r\) by \(i\)).
Proposition 3.1.4. The dual variety \(\check X\) is irreducible and proper of dimension \(\leq r-1\).
Proof. It is the image under \(\varphi\) of \(\mathbb P(N)\), itself irreducible of dimension \(r-1\) if \(X\ne\mathbb P^r\), and empty if \(X=\mathbb P^r\).

Remark 3.1.5. It is possible to make the interpretation of \(\mathbb P(N)\) more precise in the following way. Let \(Y\subset X\times\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}\) be the "incidence" subscheme of \(X\times\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}\), whose fiber at every point \(H\) of \(\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}(k)\) is \(X.H\). It is immediate that \(Y\) is smooth of dimension \(n+r-1\), as a projective bundle of relative dimension \(r-1\) over \(X\), hence \(Y\to\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}\) makes \(Y\) a relative complete intersection of virtual relative dimension \((n+r-1)-r=n-1\). This being said, the Jacobian subscheme \(J_{n-1}(Y/\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r})\) (VI 5.2) is none other than \(\mathbb P(N)\). Thanks to the compatibility of the formation of the Jacobian subscheme of a relative complete intersection with any base change, we deduce that for every line \(D\) in \(\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}\), the Jacobian subscheme \(J_{n-1}(\widetilde X/D)\), where \(\widetilde X=Y\times_{\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}}D\), is none other than \(\mathbb P(N)\times_{\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}}D\).
Proposition 3.2. The subset \(B\) of \(\check X\) formed by the hyperplanes \(H\) such that \(X.H\) has one and only one singular point, and that this point
is ordinary quadratic, is open.

This is a particular case of XV 1.3.2, relying on the theory of "henselian moduli" of R. ELKIK (XV 1.1.4). The case where one restricts to quadratic points which are non-degenerate (i.e. where \(\operatorname{car}(k)\ne2\) or \(n=\dim X\) is even) is moreover obvious thanks to 3.3 below (whose proof does not use 3.2).

Corollary 3.2.1. Under the hypotheses of 3.2, let us denote by \(F\) the closed set \(\check X-B\) of \(\check X\). Then the Lefschetz pencils form an open set in \(\operatorname{Gr}(1,\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r})\), and the following conditions are equivalent:
3.2.2. The given embedding \(i:X\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow\mathbb P^r\) is a Lefschetz embedding;
3.2.3. there exists a Lefschetz pencil of hyperplanes (for the given embedding).
3.2.4. The codimension of \(F\) in \(\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}\) is \(\geq2\).
Proof. A line \(D\) in \(\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}\) is a Lefschetz pencil if and only if it satisfies:
(3.2.5) The axis of \(D\) cuts \(X\) transversally,
(3.2.6) \(D\) is not contained in \(\check X\),
(3.2.7) \(D\) does not meet \(F\).

The conditions (3.2.5) and (3.2.6) define open sets of \(\operatorname{Gr}(1,\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r})\), and, \(F\) being closed, the same holds for (3.2.7), so the Lefschetz pencils form an open set in \(\operatorname{Gr}(1,\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r})\). Because \(\dim\check X\leq r-1\), the open set (3.2.6) is non-empty, and hence dense. The open set (3.2.5) is likewise non-empty, hence dense. Indeed, let \(H\) be a hyperplane which cuts \(X\) transversally; such an \(H\) exists since \(\dim\check X\leq r-1\). Because \(\dim(\check X\cdot G)\leq r-1\), there exists a hyperplane \(G\) which cuts \(X\cdot H\) transversally, and by construction the line \(D\) passing through \(H\) and \(G\) has an axis which cuts \(X\) transversally. Hence the embedding is a Lefschetz embedding if and only if the open set (3.2.7) is non-empty (and hence dense). Now, \(F\) being closed, (3.2.7) is non-empty if and only if \(F\) is of codimension \(\geq2\) in \(\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}\) (cf. EGA V, or Mumford, Introduction to Algebraic Geometry, p. 88, Cor 3).

Corollary 3.2.8. Under the hypotheses of 3.2., suppose that \(i:X\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow\mathbb P^r\) is a Lefschetz embedding. Let \(H_o\) be a hyperplane which cuts \(X\) transversally, and let us denote by \(\operatorname{Gr}(1,\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r})_{H_o}\) the subvariety of \(\operatorname{Gr}(1,\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r})\) formed by the lines passing through \(H_o\). Then the Lefschetz pencils passing through \(H_o\) form a dense open set in \(\operatorname{Gr}(1,\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r})_{H_o}\).
Proof. By (3.2.1), the said pencils form an open set in \(\operatorname{Gr}(1,\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r})_{H_o}\). The argument just given to

establish that the open sets (3.2.5) and (3.2.6) are non-empty shows that their intersections with \(\operatorname{Gr}(1,\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r})_{H_o}\) are likewise non-empty. It remains to prove that there are lines passing through \(H_o\) which do not meet \(F\). For this, let \(T\) be a hyperplane in \(\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}\) which does not contain \(H_o\). We have an isomorphism
\[ \operatorname{Gr}(1,\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r})_{H_o}\xleftarrow{\sim}T, \qquad \text{the line passing through }H_o\text{ and }T\longmapsfrom T, \]

so \(\operatorname{Gr}(1,\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r})_{H_o}\) "is" a projective space of dimension \(r-1\). The lines therein passing through a point of \(F\) form a closed set of dimension \(\leq r-2\) there, being the image of the morphism
\[ F\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}(1,\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r})_{H_o}, \qquad f\longmapsto\text{the line passing through }H_o\text{ and through }f. \]

Proposition 3.3. The morphism \(\varphi:\mathbb P(N)\longrightarrow\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}\) is unramified \(= non ramifié\) at a closed point \((x_o,H)\) if and only if the point \(x_o\) is a non-degenerate quadratic singularity (1.2) of \(X\cdot H\). In particular, the subset of \(\mathbb P(N)\) formed by the points \((x,H)\) such that \(x\) is a non-degenerate quadratic singularity of \(X\cdot H\) is open.

Proof. We may confine ourselves to the case \(X\ne\mathbb P^r\). The properties claimed to be equivalent being independent of the particular choice of coordinates, we can make them explicit with the help of coordinates chosen in a very particular way. Now, \(X\) being a smooth subvariety of \(\mathbb P^r\) of dimension \(n\geq1\), we can choose homogeneous coordinates \(X_o,\ldots,X_r\) in \(\mathbb P^r\) in such a way that
3.3.1. the point \(x_o\in\mathbb P^r\) is \((1,0,\ldots,0)\);

3.3.2. setting \(x_i=X_i/X_o\) for \(i=1,\ldots,r\), so that the completion \(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{\mathbb P^r,x_o}\) is identified with \(k[[x_1,\ldots,x_r]]\), the ideal defining the trace of \(X\) in \(\operatorname{Spec}\widehat{\mathcal O}_{\mathbb P^r,x_o}\) is generated by elements \(g_i\) \((i=1,\ldots,r-n)\) in \(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{\mathbb P^r,x_o}\) which, in \(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{\mathbb P^r,x_o}\), are written
\[ g_i=x_{n+i}-h_i(x_1,\ldots,x_n),\qquad i=1,\ldots,r-n, \]
where the \(h_i(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\) are series in \(x_1,\ldots,x_n\) only which lie in the square of the maximal ideal of \(k[[x_1,\ldots,x_n]]\).
3.3.3. the hyperplane \(H\) comes, via (3.1.1), from \(g_{r-n}\).
It follows from (3.3.2) that, setting \(V=\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{X,x_o})\), there is a trivialization \((I/I^2)|_V\simeq\mathcal O_V^{r-n}\)
\[ \tag{3.3.4} (\lambda_1,\ldots,\lambda_{r-n})\longmapsto\sum_1^{r-n}\lambda_i g_{n+i}. \]
It follows from this and from (3.3.3) that there exists a neighborhood \(W\) of \((x,H)\)
in \(\mathbb P(N)|_V\) such that we have \(V\times\mathbb A^{r-n-1}\simeq W\), the isomorphism being given by
\[ \tag{3.3.5} (v,(\lambda_1,\ldots,\lambda_{r-n-1}))\longmapsto \left(v,\left(g_{r-n}+\sum_1^{r-n-1}\lambda_i g_i\right)\in\mathbb P(I/I^2)|_V\right) \]
(where we set \(\lambda_i=\Lambda_i/\Lambda_{n-r}\)).

In particular, the completion \(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{\mathbb P(N),(x,H)}\) of the local ring of \(\mathbb P(N)\) at \((x,H)\) is identified with \(k[[x_1,\ldots,x_n,\lambda_1,\ldots,\lambda_{r-n-1}]]\). Let us now express \(\varphi\) in terms of these coordinates, at the level of the completions:

\[ \tag{3.3.6} \begin{aligned} \varphi(x,\lambda)&=\varphi(x_1,\ldots,x_n,\lambda_1,\ldots,\lambda_{r-n-1})\\ &=\text{the hyperplane with equation }\sum_{i=0}^{n+r}\varphi_i(x,\lambda)T_i=0. \end{aligned} \]
As the image hyperplane \(\sum\varphi_iT_i\) contains the point \((x)\), a point which is written in homogeneous coordinates
we have
\[ \tag{3.3.8} \varphi_o(x,\lambda)=-\sum_{i=1}^n x_i\varphi_i(x,\lambda)- \sum_{i=1}^{r-n}h_i(x)\varphi_{n+i}(x,\lambda) \]

in \(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{\mathbb P(N),(x_o,H)}\) and, by (3.1.1), for \(i\geq1\), setting \(D_i=\frac{\partial}{\partial x_i}\in\operatorname{Der}_k(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{\mathbb P^r,x_o},\widehat{\mathcal O}_{\mathbb P^r,x_o})\),
\[ \tag{3.3.9} \varphi_i(x,\lambda)=D_i\left(g_{r-n}+\sum_{i=1}^{r-n-1}\lambda_i g_i\right)(x) \quad\text{in }\widehat{\mathcal O}_{\mathbb P(N),(x_o,H)}. \]
Recalling \(g_i=x_{n+i}-h_i(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\), we have

\[ \tag{3.3.10} \varphi_i(x,\lambda)=-\frac{\partial}{\partial x_i} \left(h_{r-n}+\sum_{i=1}^{r-n-1}\lambda_i h_i\right) \quad\text{for }1\leq i\leq n, \] \[ \tag{3.3.11} \varphi_i(x,\lambda)=\lambda_i\quad\text{for }1\leq i\leq n, \]
and
\[ \tag{3.3.12} \varphi_r(x,\lambda)=1. \]
Let us denote by \(m\) the maximal ideal of \(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{\mathbb P(N),(x_o,H)}\). Taking into account that \(h_i\equiv0\bmod m^2\) for \(i=1,\ldots,r-n\), we find
\[ \tag{3.3.13} \varphi_o(x,\lambda)\equiv0\bmod m^2 \] \[ \tag{3.3.14} \varphi_i(x,\lambda)\equiv-\frac{\partial h_{r-n}}{\partial x_i}\bmod m^2 \quad\text{for }i=1,\ldots,n. \]
Putting together the formulas (3.3.11) - (3.3.14), we find that the Jacobian matrix of \(\varphi\) at the point \((x_o,H)\) is

\[ \tag{3.3.15} \left( \begin{array}{c|c} 0&0\\ \hline -\left(\dfrac{\partial^2h_{r-n}}{\partial x_i\partial x_j}(0)\right)_{1\leq i,j\leq n}&0\\ \hline 0&I_{r-n-1}\\ \hline 0&0 \end{array} \right). \]
Hence \(\varphi\) is unramified at \((x_o,H)\) if and only if the Hessian matrix
\[ \left(\frac{\partial^2h_{r-n}}{\partial x_i\partial x_j}(0)\right)_{1\leq i,j\leq n} \]
is invertible. On the other hand, (3.3.2) and (3.3.3) assure us that the completion of the local ring of \(x_o\) at \(X\cdot H\) is
and we conclude thanks to the definition (1.2).

Example 3.4. Suppose that \(X\) is a hypersurface in \(\mathbb P^r\), defined by a homogeneous equation \(F(X_o,\ldots,X_r)=0\). Then \(\mathbb P(N)=X\), and \(\varphi:X\longrightarrow\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}\) is expressed, in homogeneous coordinates, by
\[ \tag{3.4.1} \varphi(X_o,\ldots,X_r)=\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial X_o},\ldots, \frac{\partial F}{\partial X_r}\right). \]
This is the "GAUSS morphism".

Let us give an example where \(\varphi\) is everywhere ramified (i.e. not unramified). For this, let \(p\) be the characteristic of \(k\), \(r\) an integer \(\geq1\), and let us take the hypersurface \(X\) with equation
for which we have
\[ \tag{3.4.3} \varphi(X_o,\ldots,X_r)=(X_o^{p^r},\ldots,X_r^{p^r}), \]

so that \(\check X\) is reduced to a point if \(p=0\), and \(\check X\simeq X\), with \(\varphi\) the Frobenius morphism if \(p>0\). One will observe that it follows from 3.3 (or from 3.5.0 below) that if \(p>2\), the embedding under consideration is not a Lefschetz embedding.
Let us return to the general case.

blocks.title: Proposition 3.5.
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.5.

Suppose that the morphism \(\varphi:\mathbb P(N)\longrightarrow\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}\) is generically unramified (i.e. is not everywhere ramified). Then \(\varphi\) induces a birational morphism from \(\mathbb P(N)\) onto \(\check X\). More precisely, the three following open sets of \(\check X\) are identical:

  1. a) the largest open set of smoothness of \(\check X\),
  2. b) the largest open set \(V\) of \(\check X\) such that \(\varphi:\mathbb P(N)\longrightarrow\check X\) induces an isomorphism \(\varphi^{-1}(V)\overset\sim\longrightarrow V\),
  3. c) the open set (cf. 3.2) of \(\check X\) formed by the hyperplanes \(H\) such that \(X\cdot H\) has one and only one singular point, the latter being quadratic non-degenerate.

blocks.title: Corollary 3.5.0.
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.5.0.

Suppose \(\operatorname{car} k\ne2\) or \(\dim X\) even. The given embedding is a Lefschetz embedding if and only if one of the two following conditions is satisfied: a) \(\varphi\) is generically unramified, or b) \(\dim\check X\leq r-2\).

Indeed, the sufficiency follows from the criterion (3.2.4), for, by 3.5, we have either \(F=\operatorname{lieu\ sing}(\check X)\), hence \(\dim F<\dim(\check X)=r-1\), or \(\dim(\check X)\leq r-2\). For the necessity, one observes that if \(D\) is a Lefschetz pencil and if \(\dim\check X=r-1\), there exists \(x\in D\cap\check X\), and by 3.3 and the definition (2.2 c)), \(\varphi\) is unramified at the points of \(\varphi^{-1}(x)\), hence generically unramified, qed.
Proof of 3.5 The hypothesis implies \(\dim\check X=\dim\mathbb P(N)(=r-1)\), from which it follows (3.2) that \(\check X\) is an irreducible hypersurface in \(\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}\).

Let \(R\) be the ramification locus of \(\varphi\), which is by hypothesis a closed subset of \(\mathbb P(N)\) of dimension at most \(r-2\), so that \(\varphi(R)\) is a closed subset of \(\check X\) of dimension at most \(r-2\). Let us denote by \(V'\) the dense open set of \(\check X\)
\[ \tag{3.5.1} V'=(\check X-\varphi(R))\cap(\check X-\operatorname{lieu\ sing}(\check X)), \]
and by \(F\) its complement in \(\check X\)
\[ \tag{3.5.1.1} F=\varphi(R)\cup\operatorname{lieu\ sing}(\check X). \]

We shall first prove that \(\varphi\) induces an isomorphism of \(\varphi^{-1}(V')\) with \(V'\) by the naive method of constructing an inverse \(\psi:V'\longrightarrow\varphi^{-1}(V')\). Indeed, considering \(\varphi^{-1}(V')\) as embedded in \(X\times V'\) via (3.1.1), so that \(\varphi\) is identified with \(\operatorname{pr}_2\), we see that the construction of \(\psi\) is equivalent to the construction of a morphism \(\psi_1:V'\longrightarrow\mathbb P^r\) such that the diagram
\[ \tag{3.5.2} \begin{xy} \xymatrix{ \mathbb P(N)\supset\varphi^{-1}(V') \ar[r]^{\varphi}\ar[d] & V'\ar[d]^{\psi_1}\\ X \ar@{^{(}->}[r] & \mathbb P^r } \end{xy} \]
is commutative (we set \(\psi(v)=(\psi_1(v),v)\in\mathbb P^r\times V'\)).

For this, let us remark that for an arbitrary hypersurface in a projective space, the GAUSS morphism (3.4.1) is "defined" at every smooth point. Let us apply this to the hypersurface \(\check X\) in \(\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}\); as \(V'\) is
contained in \(V\), the open set of smoothness of \(\check X\), the GAUSS morphism gives us a morphism, denoted \(\psi_1\):
\[ \tag{3.5.3} \psi_1:V'\longrightarrow\check{\check{\mathbb P}}^{\,r}\simeq\mathbb P^r. \]

It remains to verify the commutativity of the diagram (3.5.2). Let then \((x_o,H_o)\) be a closed point of \(\varphi^{-1}(V')\); we must verify that \(x_o\), considered as being a hyperplane in \(\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}\), is tangent to \(\check X\) at \(H_o\), i.e. we shall show that for \((x_o,H_o)\in\varphi^{-1}(V')\), \(H_o\) tangent to \(X\) at \(x_o\) \(\Rightarrow x_o\) tangent to \(\check X\) at \(H_o\). As \(\varphi\) is unramified at \((x_o,H_o)\), and \(\check X\) is smooth at \(H_o=\varphi(x_o,H_o)\), \(\varphi\) induces an isomorphism of the tangent spaces
\[ \tag{3.5.4} T_{\mathbb P(N)}((x_o,H_o))\overset{\varphi}{\longrightarrow}T_{\check X}(H_o), \]
so that the first part of the proof is completed by the following lemma.

blocks.title: Lemma 3.6 (EULER).
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.6 (EULER).

Let \((x_o,H_o)\) be a closed point of \(\mathbb P(N)\), and let us denote by \(T_L\) the tangent bundle of the hyperplane \(L\) in \(\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}\) defined by \(x_o\). Then in \(T_{\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}}(H_o)\), we have

\[ \varphi\bigl(T_{\mathbb P(N)}((x_o,H_o))\bigr)\subset T_L(H_o). \]
Proof. Let us choose homogeneous coordinates \(X_o,\ldots,X_r\) in \(\mathbb P^r\), in terms of which \(x_o\) becomes the point \(A=(A_o,\ldots,A_r)\).
A point of \(T_X(x_o)\) is a point of \(\mathbb P^r\) with values in \(k[\epsilon]/(\epsilon^2)\),
\[ \tag{3.6.1} A+\epsilon B, \]
such that for every local section \(H\) of \(I\), we have
\[ \tag{3.6.2} \sum_{i=0}^r B_i\frac{\partial H}{\partial X_i}(A)=0. \]

Let us moreover choose a local section \(H\) of \(I\) which gives rise to \(H_o\) by (3.1.1). By the local triviality of \(\mathbb P(N)\) over \(X\), a point of \(T_{\mathbb P(N)}((x_o,H_o))\) is represented by a point
\[ \tag{3.6.3} (A+\epsilon B,\ H+\epsilon F), \]

where \(F\) is a local section of \(I\), and where \(A\) and \(B\) are as above. The image under \(\varphi\) of (3.6.3) is the hyperplane with coefficients in \(k[\epsilon]/(\epsilon^2)\) with equation (c.f. 3.1.1)
\[ \tag{3.6.4} \sum_{i=0}^r\frac{\partial}{\partial X_i}(H+\epsilon F)(A+\epsilon B)T_i, \]
so that the lemma is equivalent to the formula
\[ \tag{3.6.5} \sum_{i=0}^r A_i\frac{\partial}{\partial X_i}(H+\epsilon F)(A+\epsilon B)=0. \]
Taking into account that \(\epsilon^2=0\), and Taylor's formula, we have

\[ \tag{3.6.6} \frac{\partial}{\partial X_i}(H+\epsilon F)(A+\epsilon B) =\frac{\partial H}{\partial X_i}(A)+\epsilon\frac{\partial F}{\partial X_i}(A) +\epsilon\sum_{j=0}^r B_j\frac{\partial^2H}{\partial X_j\partial X_i}(A), \]
so it suffices to verify the three following formulas:

\[ \tag{3.6.7} \sum_{i=0}^r A_i\frac{\partial H}{\partial X_i}(A)=0, \] \[ \tag{3.6.8} \sum_{i=0}^r A_i\frac{\partial F}{\partial X_i}(A)=0, \] \[ \tag{3.6.9} \sum_{i,j=0}^r A_iB_j\frac{\partial^2H}{\partial X_i\partial X_j}(A)=0. \]
Now, \(H\) and \(F\) being homogeneous of degree zero, Euler's formula gives
\[ \tag{3.6.10} \sum_i X_i\frac{\partial H}{\partial X_i}=0, \qquad \sum_i X_i\frac{\partial F}{\partial X_i}=0 \]
and, \(\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial X_j}\) being homogeneous of degree \(-1\), this formula gives
\[ \sum_i X_i\frac{\partial^2H}{\partial X_i\partial X_j} =-\frac{\partial H}{\partial X_j}, \]
so that (3.6.9) can be rewritten
\[ \tag{3.6.11} -\sum_j B_j\frac{\partial H}{\partial X_j}(A)=0, \]

which is none other than (3.6.2).
This shows that \(\varphi\) induces an isomorphism
\[ \tag{3.6.12} \varphi^{-1}(V')\overset\sim\longrightarrow V'. \]
whose inverse is \(\psi\). Let us now remark that the morphism
\[ \tag{3.6.13} \begin{cases} \psi:V'\longrightarrow \mathbb P^r\times V'\subset \mathbb P^r\times\check X,\\ \psi(v)=(\psi_1(v),v) \end{cases} \]
extends to a morphism
\[ \tag{3.6.14} \begin{cases} \widetilde\psi:V\longrightarrow \mathbb P^r\times V,\\ \widetilde\psi(v)=(\widetilde\psi_1(v),v), \end{cases} \]
where we denote by
\[ \tag{3.6.15} \widetilde\psi_1:V\longrightarrow\mathbb P^r \]

the GAUSS morphism, viewed as defined on all of \(V\).
We have just shown the inclusion
\[ \tag{3.6.16} \psi(V')\subset\mathbb P(N) \]
which implies, \(V'\) being dense in \(V\), and \(\mathbb P(N)\) being closed in \(\mathbb P^r\times\check X\), the inclusion
\[ \tag{3.6.17} \widetilde\psi(V)\subset\mathbb P(N). \]
It is obvious that we even have
\[ \tag{3.6.18} \widetilde\psi(V)\subset\varphi^{-1}(V). \]
Again by "continuity", the relations
\[ \begin{cases} \psi\circ\varphi=\operatorname{id}_{\varphi^{-1}(V')},\\ \varphi\circ\psi=\operatorname{id}_{V'} \end{cases} \]
imply the relations
\[ \begin{cases} \widetilde\psi\circ\varphi=\operatorname{id}_{\varphi^{-1}(V)},\\ \varphi\circ\widetilde\psi=\operatorname{id}_V, \end{cases} \]
which shows that the open sets 3.5 d) and 3.5 e) are identical.
Let us show that the open sets 3.5 a) and 3.5 c) are identical. One remarks first that, by 3.3, the open set 3.5 c) is the subset \(U\) of \(\check X\) defined by
\[ \tag{3.6.19} U=\{x\in\check X\mid \varphi^{-1}(x)\text{ contains exactly one point, and at this point }\varphi\text{ is unramified.}\}. \]
Now \(\varphi^{-1}(U)\) is normal (being smooth) and the induced morphism
\[ \varphi:\varphi^{-1}(U)\longrightarrow U \]
is birational (we have already shown it), radicial (by definition of \(U\)) and proper. Hence
\[ \varphi:\varphi^{-1}(U)\longrightarrow U \]

is the normalization of \(U\). In particular, \(U\) is geometrically unibranch. As \(\varphi:\varphi^{-1}(U)\longrightarrow U\) is unramified, it follows (EGA IV 18.10.1) that it is étale. By EGA IV 18.10.18, we then conclude that it is an open immersion, hence an isomorphism, hence that

\(U\) is smooth, i.e. that we have the inclusion 3.5 c) \(\subset\) 3.5 a). On the other hand, (3.6.19) makes obvious the inclusion 3.5 b) \(\subset\) 3.5 c), and we conclude, taking into account that 3.5 a) = 3.5 b).
We can now prove:
3.7. Theorem 2.5 is true in the case \(\operatorname{car.}k\ne2\), as well as in the case \(X\) of even dimension.
Proof. Taking into account 3.5.0 and 3.3, it suffices to verify:

(3.7.1) For every closed point \(x_o\in X\), and every integer \(d\geq2\), there exists a hypersurface \(H\) of degree \(d\) tangent to \(X\) at \(x_o\), such that \(x_o\) is an ordinary quadratic singularity of \(X.H\).

Now, taking homogeneous coordinates \(X_o,\ldots,X_r\) suitably, we may assume that \(x_o\) is the point \((1,0,\ldots,0)\), and that the functions \(X_i/X_o\), \(i=1,\ldots,n\), give local coordinates on \(X\) in a neighborhood of \(x_o\). We take for \(H\) the hypersurface with equation

\[ \tag{3.7.1.1}H=X_o^{d-2}\sum_{i=1}^nX_i^2\qquad\text{if }\operatorname{car.}(k)\ne2, \] \[ \tag{3.7.1.2}H=X_o^{d-2}\sum_{i=1}^{n/2}X_iX_{n/2+i}\qquad\text{if }n\text{ is even}, \] \[ \tag{3.7.1.3}H=X_o^{d-2}\left(X_n^2+\sum_{i=1}^{[n/2]}X_iX_{[n/2]+i}\right)\qquad\text{if }n\text{ is odd}. \]

For the statement 2.5.2, let us remark that either \(\varphi\) is generically unramified, and 3.5.0 applies, or \(\varphi\) is everywhere ramified, which implies \(\dim X\leq r-2\), \(k\) being of characteristic zero. In this last case, the criterion (3.2.4) is trivially verified.
4. The general case
We shall sketch another method of proof of 2.5 valid in every characteristic, admitting the following statement, close to 3.1, and a particular case of XV 1.1.4.
(4.1) The subset of \(\mathbb P(N)\) formed by the points \((x,H)\) such that \(x\) is an ordinary quadratic singularity of \(X.H\) is open.
Let us remark that 4.1 follows from 3.3, except if \(\operatorname{car}(k)=2\) and \(\dim X\) odd.
4.1.1. Let us denote by \(R_1\) the closed subset of \(\mathbb P(N)\) complement of the open set 4.1, and by \(F_1\) its image \(\varphi(R_1)\subset\check X\subset\check{\mathbb P}^{,r}\).
Lemma 4.1.2. If the open set 4.1 is non-empty, then \(\operatorname{codim}_{\check{\mathbb P}^{,r}}(F_1)\geq2\).
Proof. By hypothesis, \(\dim(R_1)<\dim\mathbb P(N)=r-1\), and \(F_1=\varphi(R_1)\).
4.1.3. Let us denote by \(F_2\) the part of \(\check X\) formed by the hyperplanes which are tangent to \(X\) at at least two points. One remarks that \(F_2\) is
not closed, but that we have
\[ \tag{4.1.4}\text{the closure of }F_2\subset F_1\cup F_2, \]

this because of the fact that \(F_1\cup F_2=F\) (cf. 3.2) is closed. (The inclusion 4.1.4. "says" that a confluence of two singularities is not ordinary quadratic.) In any case the construction of 4.2.4. will show that \(F_2\) is constructible. To prove 2.5.1 we shall apply the criterion 3.2.4. For this, we shall need to know that \(\operatorname{codim}_{\check{\mathbb P}^{,r}}(F_1)\geq2\) and that \(\operatorname{codim}_{\check{\mathbb P}^{,r}}(F_2)\geq2\). On the other hand, 3.8.1 and 4.1.2 assure us that, replacing the given embedding by its \(d\)-th multiple, \(d\geq2\), we shall have \(\operatorname{codim}_{\check{\mathbb P}^{,r}}(F_2)\geq2\). We conclude that 2.5.1 will be proved, once we know the

Proposition 4.2. Let \(X\) be a smooth irreducible subvariety of \(\mathbb P^r\), of dimension \(n\geq1\). For every integer \(d\geq2\), the part \(F_2\) of \(\check{\mathbb P}^{\,N}=\mathbb P^{\binom{n+d}{d}-1}\) formed by the hypersurfaces of degree \(d\) in \(\mathbb P^r\) which are tangent to \(X\) at at least two points is of codimension \(\geq2\) in \(\check{\mathbb P}^{\,N}\).

4.2.1. Let us begin with the special case \(d=2\) and \(X\) a linear subvariety of \(\mathbb P^r\). Now, it will suffice to find a line in \(\check{\mathbb P}^{\,N}\) which does not meet \(F\) (cf. 3.2.4). Let us choose homogeneous coordinates \(X_o,\ldots,X_r\) in \(\mathbb P^r\) in such a way that \(X\) is defined by
\(X_{n+1}=\cdots=X_r=0\), and homogeneous coordinates \((\lambda,\mu)\) in \(\mathbb P^1\).
For \(n\) even, \(n=2k\), we take the pencil of quadrics
\[ \tag{4.2.2}H_{(\lambda,\mu)}=\lambda X_o^2+(\lambda X_k+\mu X_o)X_{2k} +\sum_{i=1}^{k-1}(\lambda X_i+\mu X_{i+1})X_{k+i}\ ; \]
for \(n\) odd, \(n=2k+1\), we take the pencil
\[ \tag{4.2.3}H_{(\lambda,\mu)}=(\lambda X_1+\mu X_o)X_o +\sum_{i=1}^{k}(\lambda X_{2i}+\mu X_{i})X_{2i+1}\ . \]

In both cases, one observes that \(X\mathbin{\cdot}H_{(\lambda,\mu)}\) is smooth for \((\lambda,\mu)\ne(0,1)\), and that \(X\mathbin{\cdot}H_{(0,1)}\) has only one singular point, which is even ordinary quadratic, and hence that the pencil does not cut \(F\).

4.2.4. Let us denote by \(\operatorname{Btg}^d(X)\) the subvariety of \(\check{\mathbb P}^{\,N}\times(X\times X-\Delta(X\times X))\) formed by the triples \((H,x_1,x_2)\) such that \(H\) is a hypersurface of degree \(d\) in \(\mathbb P^r\) which is tangent to \(X\) at \(x_1\) and at \(x_2\), and \(x_1\ne x_2\). The image of \(\operatorname{Btg}^d(X)\) under \(\operatorname{pr}_1\) is the set \(F_2\) of (4.2), so that it will suffice to prove
\[ \tag{4.2.5}\dim\operatorname{Btg}^d(X)\leq N-2\quad\text{if }d\geq3,\text{ or if }d=2\text{ and }X\text{ is not linear.} \]
For this, let us denote by \(\operatorname{Lin}(X\times X)\) the subvariety of \(X\times X-\Delta(X\times X)\)

\[ \tag{4.2.6}\begin{aligned} \operatorname{Lin}(X\times X) &=\{(x_1,x_2)\in X\times X\mid x_1\ne x_2,\ \text{and the line generated by }x_1,x_2\\ &\hspace{8em}\text{is tangent to }X\text{ at }x_1\}\\ &=\{(x_1,x_2)\in X\times X\mid x_1\ne x_2\text{ and }x_2\in X\cap\operatorname{Tg}_X(x_1)\}. \end{aligned} \]
We obviously have:
4.2.7. If \(X\) is not a linear subvariety of \(\mathbb P^r\), then
\[ \dim\operatorname{Lin}(X\times X)<2\dim X. \]
Hence for (4.2.5) it will suffice to prove that the fibers of the morphism
\[ \tag{4.2.8}\operatorname{Btg}^d(X)\xrightarrow{\operatorname{pr}_{2,3}}X\times X-\Delta(X\times X), \]
considered as subvarieties of \(\check{\mathbb P}^{\,N}\), are

\[ \tag{4.2.9}\left\{ \begin{array}{ll} \text{of codimension }\geq2\dim(X)+1&\text{above }\operatorname{Lin}(X\times X),\\ \text{of codimension }\geq2\dim(X)+2&\text{above }X\times X-\Delta(X\times X)-\operatorname{Lin}(X\times X). \end{array}\right. \]
The statement (4.2.9) is a consequence of the following lemma, taking for \(L_i\) the tangent space to \(X\) at \(x_i\), \(i=1,2\).

Proposition 4.3. Let \(x_1\) and \(x_2\) be two distinct closed points of \(\mathbb P^r\), and \(L_1\) and \(L_2\) two linear subspaces of \(\mathbb P^r\) such that \(x_1\in L_1\) and \(x_2\in L_2\). In the space \(\check{\mathbb P}^{\,N}=\check{\mathbb P}_d\) of hypersurfaces of degree \(d\), the linear subspace formed by the hypersurfaces tangent to \(L_1\) at \(x_1\) and to \(L_2\) at \(x_2\) is of codimension

\[ \left\{\begin{array}{ll} =\dim L_1+\dim L_2+2&\text{if }d\geq3,\text{ or if }d=2\text{ and }L_1\cap L_2\text{ does not contain}\\ &\text{the line }(x_1,x_2)\text{ containing }x_1,x_2,\\[2pt] =\dim L_1+\dim L_2+1&\text{if }d=2\text{ and }L_1\cap L_2\text{ contains the line }(x_1,x_2). \end{array}\right. \]

Proof. For \(d\geq3\), we must prove that \(\dim L_1+\dim L_2+2\) linear conditions are independent, so that it suffices to treat the case \(L_1=L_2=\mathbb P^r\), i.e. to find "how many" hypersurfaces are singular at \(x_1\) and at \(x_2\). Now, in terms of homogeneous coordinates chosen in such a way that \(x_1=(1,0,\ldots,0)\) and \(x_2=(0,1,0,\ldots,0)\), the hypersurface with equation \(\sum A_WX^W\) is singular at \((1,0,\ldots,0)\) if and only if the coefficients of the monomials \(X_o^{d-1}X_i\), \(i=0,\ldots,r\), are all zero, and it is singular at \((0,1,0,\ldots,0)\) if and only if the coefficients of the monomials \(X_1^{d-1}X_i\), \(i=0,\ldots,r\), are all zero. For \(d\geq3\), the monomials \(X_o^{d-1}X_i\), \(i=0,\ldots,r\) and \(X_1^{d-1}X_i\), \(i=0,\ldots,r\) are all distinct, which gives the desired statement for \(d\geq3\).
For \(d=2\), the same calculation gives the inequality
\[ \operatorname{codim}\geq\dim L_1+\dim L_2+1, \]
(for the monomial \(X_oX_1\) is counted twice).

Let us verify that if \(L_1\cap L_2\) does not contain the line \((x_1,x_2)\), then the tangency conditions are independent. We reduce to the case \(L_1=\mathbb P^r\), \(L_2\) a hyperplane in \(\mathbb P^r\) which does not contain the line \((x_1,x_2)\). We choose homogeneous coordinates \(X_o,\ldots,X_r\) in such a way that \(x_1=(1,0,\ldots,0)\), \(x_2=(0,1,0,\ldots,0)\) and \(L_2\) is the hyperplane \(X_o=0\). Now, a quadric \(\sum_{i\leq j}A_{ij}X_iX_j\) is singular at \((1,0,\ldots,0)\) if and only if \(A_{oj}=0\) for \(j=0,\ldots,r\), and tangent to
\(X_o=0\) at \((0,1,\ldots,0)\) if and only if \(A_{1j}=0\) for \(j=1,\ldots,r\), which together makes \(2r+1=\dim L_1+\dim L_2+2\) independent conditions.

For the case where \(L_1\cap L_2\) contains the line \((x_1,x_2)\), we reduce to the case \(L_1=L_2=\) the line \((x_1,x_2)\). In terms of homogeneous coordinates in which \(x_1=(1,0,\ldots,0)\), \(x_2=(0,1,0,\ldots,0)\), the quadric \(\sum_{i\leq j}A_{ij}X_iX_j\) passes through \((1,0,\ldots,0)\) and is tangent there to \(L_1\) if and only if \(A_{oo}=A_{o1}=0\); it passes through \((0,1,0,\ldots,0)\) and is tangent there to \(L_2\) if and only if \(A_{11}=A_{o1}=0\), which together makes only \(3=\dim L_1+\dim L_2+1\) conditions.

pages.title: 5. The degree of the dual variety by an "elementary" route

blocks.html: 5. The degree of the dual variety by an "elementary" route

(Cf. XVIII 3.2. for another method in the case where \(\dim X\)
is even or \(\operatorname{car}(k)\ne2\).)
We still employ the notation of 2.2, so \(X\longrightarrow\mathbb P^r\) is a smooth irreducible subvariety of dimension \(n\).
5.1. Let us begin by considering the morphism (3.1.1)
\[ \tag{5.1.0}\nu:\mathbb P(N)\longrightarrow X\times\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r} \]
from a more "functorial" point of view. (I wish to thank S. Kleiman for having pointed out to me the formulation which follows.) We have an exact sequence
of coherent sheaves on \(X\)
\[ \tag{5.1.1}0\longrightarrow\check N\longrightarrow\Omega^1_{\mathbb P/k}|X\longrightarrow\Omega^1_X\longrightarrow0. \]
On the other hand, "writing the differential forms on \(\mathbb P^r\) in homogeneous coordinates", one finds an exact sequence of sheaves on \(\mathbb P^r\)
\[ \tag{5.1.2}0\longrightarrow\Omega^1_{\mathbb P^r/k}\longrightarrow(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P}(-1))^{r+1}\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{\mathbb P}\longrightarrow0 \]
which, restricted to \(X\), then gives an exact sequence
\[ \tag{5.1.3}0\longrightarrow\Omega^1_{\mathbb P^r/k}|X\longrightarrow(\mathcal O_X(-1))^{r+1}\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{\mathbb P}\longrightarrow0. \]
Putting together 5.1.1 and 5.1.3, we find an inclusion
\[ \tag{5.1.4}\check N\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow(\mathcal O_X(-1))^{r+1}, \]
which gives, by transposition, a surjection,
\[ \tag{5.1.5}\mathcal O_X(1)^{r+1}\longrightarrow N \]
whence a closed immersion
\[ \tag{5.1.6}\alpha:\mathbb P(N)\longrightarrow\mathbb P(\mathcal O_X(1)^{r+1})\simeq X\times\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r} \]
which is none other than (5.1.0).
5.2. The idea of the calculation.

We suppose from now on that \(\check X=\varphi(\mathbb P(N))\) is of dimension \(r-1\), hence that it is a hypersurface in \(\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}\). The calculation of its degree is done naturally by introducing the Chern classes [1].

For every smooth projective variety \(T\), we denote by \(\operatorname{CH}(T)\) its Chow ring, which is a graded ring (by the codimension of a cycle). Given an element \(z\in\operatorname{CH}(T)\), we denote by

the degree of its component of degree zero (which is a 0-cycle). The class in \(\operatorname{CH}^1(T)\) of an invertible sheaf \(L\) on \(T\) will also be denoted \(L\) when no confusion is to be feared (for example, when it lies under the integration sign \(\int_T\)).
This being said, denoting by \(L\) the class in \(\operatorname{CH}(\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r})\) of a line, we have
\[ \tag{5.2.1}\deg(\check X)=\int_{\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}}L\cdot\check X. \]
Now, \(\check X\) is "set-theoretically" the image of \(\mathbb P(N)\) under \(\varphi\) (3.1.2). But, from the point of view of the "direct image" homomorphism
\[ \varphi_*:\operatorname{CH}(\mathbb P(N))\longrightarrow\operatorname{CH}(\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}) \]
we have
\[ \varphi_*(\mathbb P(N))=\deg(\varphi)\cdot\check X, \]
so that 5.2.1 can be rewritten
\[ \tag{5.2.2}\deg(\check X)=\frac1{\deg(\varphi)}\int_{\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}}L\cdot\varphi_*(\mathbb P(N)); \]

by abuse of notation, in \(\deg(\varphi)\), \(\varphi\) obviously denotes the morphism \(\mathbb P(N)\longrightarrow\check X\) induced by (3.1.2), which is an integer \(\geq0\), zero if and only if \(\dim\check X<r-1\) \(= (\dim\mathbb P(N))\). By the projection formula for \(\varphi\), this can be rewritten
\[ \tag{5.2.3}\deg(\check X)=\frac1{\deg(\varphi)}\int_{\mathbb P(N)}\varphi^*(L). \]
Let us denote by \(\eta\in\operatorname{CH}(\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r})\) the class of a hyperplane, so that \(L=\eta^{r-1}\), and (5.2.3) can be rewritten
\[ \tag{5.2.4}\deg(\check X)=\frac1{\deg(\varphi)}\int_{\mathbb P(N)}(\varphi^*(\eta))^{r-1}. \]
Let us now calculate the class \(\varphi^*(\eta)\) in "concrete" terms.

Let us denote by \(\xi\) (resp. \(\tau\)) the canonical invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P(N)}(1)\) on \(\mathbb P(N)\) (resp. \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P(\mathcal O_X(1)^{r+1})}(1)\) on \(\mathbb P(\mathcal O_X(1)^{r+1})\)). By functoriality, we have (cf. (5.1.6))
On the other hand, by means of the identification \(\mathbb P(\mathcal O_X(1)^{r+1})\simeq X\times\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}\), we obviously have, setting \(H=\mathcal O_X(1)\),
\[ \tag{5.2.6}\tau=\operatorname{pr}_1^*(H)\otimes\operatorname{pr}_2^*(\eta). \]
Let us denote by \(\pi:\mathbb P(N)\longrightarrow X\) the structural morphism; we have
\[ \left\{\begin{aligned} \pi&=\operatorname{pr}_1\circ\alpha,\\ \varphi&=\operatorname{pr}_2\circ\alpha, \end{aligned}\right. \]
whence, applying \(\alpha^*\) to both sides of (5.2.6), we find, by (5.2.5)
\[ \tag{5.2.6'}\xi=\alpha^*(\tau)=\pi^*(H)\otimes\varphi^*(\eta). \]
In \(\operatorname{CH}(\mathbb P(N))\), we thus have
and (5.2.3) can be rewritten
\[ \tag{5.2.8}\deg(\check X)=\frac1{\deg(\varphi)}\int_{\mathbb P(N)}(\xi-\pi^*(H))^{r-1}. \]
The second member of 5.2.8 "can be calculated" thanks to the following lemma (cf. 5.3.3):

Lemma 5.3. Let \(X\) be a quasi-projective, smooth and connected variety of dimension \(n\), and \(M\) a locally free sheaf of finite rank \(a\) on \(X\). Let us denote by \(\xi\) the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P(M)}(1)\) on \(\mathbb P(M)\),

and by \(\pi:\mathbb P(M)\longrightarrow X\) the structural morphism. We know that \(\operatorname{CH}(\mathbb P(M))\) is a \(\operatorname{CH}(X)\)-module (via \(\pi^*\)) which is free, with basis \(1,\xi,\ldots,\xi^{a-1}\), where \(\xi\) satisfies the equation
the \(C_i\) denoting the Chern classes in \(\operatorname{CH}(X)\) of \(M\). We set \(C(M)=1+C_1+C_2+\cdots\), the total Chern class of \(M\); it is an invertible element of \(\operatorname{CH}(X)\).
Then:
(i) We have the formula
\[ \tag{5.3.2}\pi_*\left(\frac{\xi^{a-1}}{1+\xi}\right)=\frac1{C(M)}. \]

(ii) Let \(F(T)\in\operatorname{CH}(X)[T]\) be a polynomial with coefficients in \(\operatorname{CH}(X)\), such that the coefficient of \(T^i\) is a homogeneous element of degree \(n+a-1-i\). Thus \(F(\xi)\) is an element of "maximum" degree \(n+a-1=\dim\mathbb P(M)\) in \(\operatorname{CH}(\mathbb P(M))\). This being said, we have
Proof. Let us begin by deducing (5.3.3) from (5.3.2).
Let us write
\[ \tag{5.3.4}\frac1{C(M)}=\sum_{j\geq0}b_j,\qquad b_0=1,\quad b_j\text{ of degree }j\text{ in }\operatorname{CH}(X), \qquad b_j=0\text{ for }j<0, \]
so that (5.3.2) can be rewritten
(taking into account that \(\pi_*(\xi^j)=0\) if \(j<a-1\) for reasons of degree).

Proof. We apply (5.3.3) (for \(M=N\), the normal sheaf of \(X\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow\mathbb P^r\)) to (5.2.8).
Let us write, for a quasi-projective smooth variety \(S\), its total Chern class (cf. 5.3)

The dual of the exact sequence (5.1.2) shows that we have

(5.7) For a smooth projective variety \(S\), we define its Euler-Poincaré characteristic by
Proposition 5.7.2. Let us denote by \(Y\) a smooth hyperplane section of \(X\), and by \(\Delta\) a smooth section of \(X\) by a linear subspace of \(\mathbb P^r\) of codimension two. We have
\[ \tag{5.7.3}\deg(\check X)=\frac{(-1)^n}{\deg(\varphi)} \bigl[\chi(X)+\chi(\Delta)-2\chi(Y)\bigr]. \]
Proof. Let us denote by \(i:Y\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow X\) the inclusion. We have
\[ N_{Y\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow X}=H|Y=i^*H, \]
so that the exact sequence
\[ 0\longrightarrow T_Y\longrightarrow i^*T_X\longrightarrow i^*H\longrightarrow0 \]
gives
\[ \tag{5.7.4}C(Y)=i^*\left(\frac{C(X)}{1+H}\right). \]
We thus have
\[ \chi(Y)=\int_Y i^*\left(\frac{C(X)}{1+H}\right) \]
and, applying the projection formula
\[ \tag{5.7.5}\chi(Y)=\int_X i_*i^*\left(\frac{C(X)}{1+H}\right) =\int_X\frac{H\cdot C(X)}{1+H}. \]
Iterating this calculation, we find for \(\Delta\)
\[ \tag{5.7.6}\chi(\Delta)=\int_Y\frac{H\,C(Y)}{1+H} =\int_Y i^*\left(\frac{H}{1+H}\cdot\frac{C(X)}{1+H}\right) =\int_X\frac{H^2C(X)}{(1+H)^2}, \]
so that
\[ \begin{aligned} \chi(X)-2\chi(Y)+\chi(\Delta) &=\int_X C(X)\left[1-\frac{2H}{1+H}+\frac{H^2}{(1+H)^2}\right]\\ &=\int_X\frac{C(X)}{(1+H)^2}, \end{aligned} \]
which completes the proof, taking into account 5.6.1.

pages.title: 6. The case of a general base

blocks.html: 6. The case of a general base

6.1. Let \(S\) be a scheme and \(\bar s\) a geometric point of \(S\), defined by an algebraic closure of the residue field \(k(s)\) for \(s\in S\). Let us denote by \(\mathbb P^r\) the projective space of \(r\) dimensions over \(S\), by \(\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}\) the dual projective space and by \(I\subset\mathbb P^r\times_S\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}\) the incidence variety. Let \(D\subset\check{\mathbb P}^{\,r}\) be a line defined by a point with values in \(S\) of a suitable Grassmannian, and \(\Delta\) the axis of the corresponding pencil of hyperplanes (cf. 2.1).

Let \(X\) be a proper smooth \(S\)-scheme, purely of dimension \(n\geq1\), equipped with a projective embedding \(X\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow\mathbb P^r\). Let \(\widetilde X=(X\times_S D)\cap I\) be the scheme over \(D\) whose fibers are the hyperplane sections of \(X\) parametrized by \(D\), and \(u\) the projection of \(\widetilde X\) onto \(D\).
Let us denote by an index \(\bar s\) the base change by \(\bar s\longrightarrow S\).

Suppose that \(\Delta_{\bar s}\) is transverse to \(X_{\bar s}\). Then, above a neighborhood \(U\) of \(s\) in \(S\), \(\Delta\) is transverse to \(X\). We suppose in what follows that \(U=S\). The scheme \(\widetilde X\) is then smooth over \(S\), and a neighborhood of the inverse image of \(\Delta\) in \(\widetilde X\) is smooth over \(D\).

Suppose moreover that one of the hyperplane sections of \(X_{\bar s}\) belonging to the pencil \(D_{\bar s}\) is smooth. Then, after replacing \(S\) by a suitable neighborhood of \(s\), the subscheme \(F\) of \(\widetilde X\) defined by the Jacobian ideal \(J^{n-1}(\widetilde X/D)\) is finite and flat over \(S\).

6.2. The exceptional locus of the pencil \(D\) is the relative Cartier divisor \(E\) of \(D/S\) defined by the \(\mathcal O_D\)-Module \(u_*\mathcal O_F\), finite and flat over \(S\). Locally on \(D\), if
\[ \mathcal O_D^n\xrightarrow{v}\mathcal O_D^n\longrightarrow u_*\mathcal O_F\longrightarrow0 \]
is a presentation of \(u_*\mathcal O_F\), \(E\) has equation \(\det(v)=0\). The formation of \(E\) is compatible with any base change.

The divisor \(E_{\bar s}\) of \(D_{\bar s}\) is written \(\sum n(P)\cdot P\), for \(n(P)\) the sum of the colengths of the Jacobian ideal \(J^{n-1}(\widetilde X/D)\) at the critical points of \(u\) with image \(P\).

blocks.title: Proposition 6.3.
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.3.

If \(D_{\bar s}\) is a Lefschetz pencil of hyperplane sections of \(X_{\bar s}\), and if \(\operatorname{car}k(s)\ne2\) or \(n\) is even, there exists a neighborhood \(U\) of \(s\) in \(S\) such that

\(a\) For every geometric point \(\bar t\) of \(U\), \(D_{\bar t}\) is a Lefschetz pencil of hyperplane sections of \(X_{\bar t}\).

\(b\) Above \(U\), the exceptional locus \(E\) is étale over \(S\).

To prove \(a\) and \(b\), it suffices to note that, under the hypotheses made, \(D_{\bar s}\) satisfies condition c) of 2.2 if and only if the divisor \(E_{\bar s}\) is reduced. One could, by invoking 3.3, use only the easier half (\(\Longrightarrow\)) of this equivalence.

6.3. The proposition becomes false in the exceptional case cited. However, if \(S\) is of characteristic 2 and \(n\) is odd, there exists a neighborhood \(U\) of \(S\) above which \(a\) is true, and one can define a divisor \(E'\) étale over \(U\) such that \(E=2E'\). We shall make no use of this result.
References

[1] GROTHENDIECK, A. La théorie des classes de Chern,
Bull. Soc. Math. France, 86, 1958, 137–154.
